candidates.reasoning: The wide, flat prairie landscape with concrete slab road construction and specific utility pole styles is highly characteristic of the Great Plains region. The vegetation and open horizon are consistent with the central United States.

The prairie terrain and road infrastructure are very similar to the Canadian Prairies (Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba), which share a nearly identical physical environment with the northern US plains.
The flat pampas region features similar grass-dominated landscapes and wide-open horizons, though the specific road materials and utility poles are less typical than in North America.

The landscape shows a mix of open grasslands and scrubby, low-canopy trees characteristic of the Pampas or Chaco regions. The vegetation density and species profile are consistent with the agricultural plains of central Argentina.

The rolling grasslands and similar scrub vegetation are highly characteristic of the Uruguayan countryside. It is botanically indistinguishable from the eastern Pampas of Argentina in this specific view.
The vegetation is consistent with the Cerrado or Pampa biomes in southern Brazil, though the lack of distinct tropical markers makes it less certain than the Southern Cone options.

The combination of concrete road slabs with dark sealant in the joints and a yellow outer edge line is very common on Mexican highways. The driving side is right-hand traffic, which is consistent with North American standards.
The road construction (concrete slabs) and the use of yellow edge lines are standard in many US states. It shares these infrastructure characteristics with Mexico and Canada.
Right-hand traffic and concrete highway construction with yellow shoulder markings are consistent with Canadian road standards, particularly in prairie provinces.

The concrete road slabs with dark sealant in the joints are very common on Mexican highways. The utility poles are simple concrete posts without complex cross-arms, which is a standard design across rural Mexico.
Concrete highways with similar joint patterns exist in the southern US, but the specific style of the utility poles is more characteristic of Latin American infrastructure.

evidence: concrete road slabs with dark sealant

country_result:
Country: Mexico
Coordinates: 23.6346, -102.5534
Reasoning: The most decisive evidence is the continuous yellow outer edge line on the right side of the road, which is a strong diagnostic marker for Mexican federal highways, whereas the US and Canada use white lines for right-hand shoulders. This is further supported by the presence of tapered circular concrete utility poles and concrete road slabs with dark bitumen sealant, both of which are highly characteristic of rural Mexican infrastructure. The flat, scrubby landscape is consistent with the Mexican Altiplano.
